Variables.desc: Assign a value to a variable (no spaces)
Variables.details: Initializes a variable. In Bash, there must be no spaces around the '=' sign.

Variables.desc: Print the value of a variable
Variables.details: Accesses and displays the value stored in a variable by prefixing its name with '$'.
Defines a constant variable that cannot be changed or unset later in the script.
Removes a variable from the current shell execution environment.

Variables.desc: Use default if VAR is unset/empty
Variables.details: A parameter expansion that provides a fallback value if the variable is null or unset.
Makes the variable available to any child processes (subshells) started from the current shell.

Special Variables.details: Contains the name of the script as it was invoked. Useful for displaying usage information.

Special Variables.desc: The first, second, etc. positional arguments
Special Variables.details: Variables that hold the arguments passed to the script or function by their position.

Special Variables.desc: The total number of arguments passed
Special Variables.details: Expands to the number of positional parameters in decimal.

Special Variables.desc: All arguments as a list of strings
Special Variables.details: Expands to all positional parameters. When double-quoted, each parameter expands to a separate word.
Special Variables.example: for arg in "$@"; do echo "$arg"; done

Special Variables.desc: Exit status of the last executed command
Special Variables.details: Holds the exit status of the most recently executed foreground pipeline (0 for success, non-zero for error).

Special Variables.desc: Process ID (PID) of the current script
Special Variables.details: The process ID of the current shell. Useful for creating unique temporary files.
Environment variable containing the username of the current user.

Conditionals.details: The classic 'test' command based if-statement. Square brackets are actually a command called '['.

Conditionals.cmd: if [ <cond> ]; then ... elif [ <cond> ]; then ... else ... fi
Conditionals.details: Provides multi-branch logic based on multiple conditions.
Test if the string length is zero. Always quote variables to prevent errors if they are empty.

Conditionals.desc: True if string is not empty
Conditionals.details: Test if the string length is non-zero.

Conditionals.desc: True if strings are not equal
True if integers are equal (-ne for not equal)
Conditionals.details: Numeric comparison operator for equality. Use -ne for 'not equal'.

Conditionals.desc: True if A > B (-lt for A < B, -ge for >=, -le for <=)
Conditionals.details: Numeric comparison operators: -gt (greater than), -lt (less than), -ge (greater or equal), -le (less or equal).

File Tests.desc: True if file or directory exists
File Tests.details: Checks for the existence of a file or directory regardless of its type.

File Tests.desc: True if it is a regular file
File Tests.details: Checks if the path exists and is a regular file (not a directory or device).

File Tests.desc: True if it is a directory
File Tests.details: Checks if the path exists and is a directory.
Checks if the file exists and has a size greater than zero bytes.
Checks if the file exists and execute permission is granted.

Loops.desc: For loop over a range of numbers
Loops.details: Iterates through a sequence of numbers generated by brace expansion.
Loops.example: for i in {1..5}; do echo $i; done

Loops.desc: For loop over files in a directory
Loops.details: Uses globbing to iterate over all files matching the pattern.
Loops.example: for f in *.log; do mv "$f" "$f.bak"; done

Loops.details: Executes the loop body as long as the condition remains true.

Loops.desc: Until loop (runs until condition is true)
Loops.details: Executes the loop body as long as the condition is false, stopping once it becomes true.
Immediately terminates the innermost loop and transfers control to the statement following the loop.

Loops.desc: Skip the rest of the loop iteration
Loops.details: Skips the remaining commands in the current loop iteration and starts the next one.

Functions.details: The standard way to define a function in POSIX-compliant shells and Bash.
Bash-specific syntax for defining functions. Does not require parentheses if 'function' keyword is used.
Functions are called like commands, and arguments are passed as positional parameters $1, $2, etc.
Terminates the function and returns the specified exit status to the caller. 0 typically means success.

Arrays.details: Initializes a Bash array with multiple elements.
Retrieves the element at the specified index. Bash arrays are zero-indexed.
Expands to all elements in the array. Quoting "${arr[@]}" preserves spaces within elements.

Arrays.desc: Get the length of the array
Arrays.details: Expands to the number of elements in the array.

Arrays.desc: Append an element to the array
Arrays.details: Adds a new element to the end of an existing array.

Arithmetic.details: The 'let' command allows arithmetic evaluation on shell variables.
Preferred modern syntax for integer arithmetic that expands to the result of the calculation.
An external utility used for arithmetic in older scripts. Generally slower than built-in expansion.
